attributes.stem: A 65-year-old man presents to the GP with a three week history of the intermittent passage of blood in his urine. The blood is mixed in with the urine and not associated with any pain. He is otherwise well and not on any medications. Physical examination is normal and digital rectal examination finds a moderate degree of smooth enlargement of both lobes of the prostate. Urinalysis and urine culture are arranged and a urinary tract infection ruled out.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has a three week history of frank haematuria. In all cases of macroscopic haematuria, benign causes must be assessed, with preliminary investigations such as urinalysis and urine dipstick performed, as in this stem.

Following a negative urine culture, malignancy must be considered and excluded. This patient will need pelvic and abdominal imaging, as well as cystoscopy. In practice, as this patient has presented to his GP, it will be easiest to get some imaging performed before the patient sees a urologist. A CT urogram with intravenous contrast is required for patients with gross haematuria, as the IV contrast lines the collecting system and ureter which will identify any filling defect, such as a urothelial carcinoma, as well as a solid renal tumour or urinary tract calculi. While cystoscopy and other invasive investigations are the more sensitive for detecting malignancy, it is most feasible for the GP to organise for imaging while the former is being organised, therefore making CT urogram with IV contrast the most appropriate answer.

CT urogram non contrast is used to look for urinary tract calculi and may miss other pathology such as a solid renal tumour or urothelial carcinoma.

Renal US is used to investigate microscopic haematuria, as it is relatively non invasive, uses no radiation, and identifies solid renal tumours, or hydronephrosis which may suggest a stone or urothelial carcinoma. If these are seen, then a CT Urogram with iv contrast is then required.

MRI abdomen and pelvis is not an appropriate first line investigation tool.

Cystoscopy will be needed to investigate the lower urinary tract, however first of all it must be determined whether there is any upper tract pathology such as suspicion of a urothelial carcinoma. If an upper tract issue is identified, then ureteroscopy can be performed at the same time as cystoscopy.
learningPoints: Renal cell carcinoma should be considered and excluded in all patients with persistent frank haematuria.
